apps.name: Pairadox
apps.summary: Mix two ingredients into a creative challenge or a funny story with a three-panel symbolic storyboard.
apps.description: A tactile idea game with challenge and story modes. Drag, tap, or use the keyboard to load two ingredients, then create a prompt or a complete local-only comedy with animated symbolic scenes and optional sound.

config.instructions: Drag or tap two ingredients into the forge. Choose Challenge for a prompt or Story for a generated comedy with symbolic scenes, then remix whenever you want another result.
outcomes: Invent a game that uses both ingredients and explain it in one sentence.
Create a thirty-second story in which both ingredients solve the same problem.
Design a fictional product inspired by both ingredients and give it a slogan.
Make a harmless challenge for a friend that includes both ingredients.

apps.name: Time Sense
apps.summary: Choose a quick or deep session and test how accurately you feel time passing.
apps.description: An arcade-like perception game with three session lengths, a moving canvas spark, hidden timing, optional local sound, scoring, touch, pointer, keyboard support, and clean resource teardown.

config.instructions: Choose Quick, Classic, or Endurance, then start the hidden timer and stop when the target duration feels complete.

apps.name: Fair Choice
apps.summary: Spin an animated fairness wheel that gently reduces repeat winners.
apps.description: A responsive canvas wheel for chores, turns, restaurants, or group choices. It supports touch, pointer, keyboard, optional audio, recent-winner balancing, duplicate protection, and clean cancellation when closed.

config.instructions: Add two to twelve choices, then tap the animated wheel or use the keyboard. Recent winners become temporarily less likely to repeat.

apps.name: Tiny Step
apps.summary: Build a moving three-step trail matched to your goal and current energy.
apps.description: A local-only momentum tool with an animated SVG trail, draggable step selector, touch and keyboard controls, optional audio, flexible completion tracking, and energy-aware actions.

config.instructions: Name the goal, choose your real energy level, then build a three-step trail. Drag the marker, tap a step, or use the keyboard to inspect and complete the route.

apps.name: Constraint Spark
apps.summary: Draw freely or race through three timed creative constraints with one strategic swap.
apps.description: A tactile 16-card constraint deck with Explore and Three-card Sprint modes, 30/60/90-second pacing, scoring, one strategic swap, animated SVG motion, touch, pointer, keyboard controls, optional local audio, and no-repeat reshuffling.

config.instructions: Explore the animated deck freely, or choose a 30, 60, or 90-second three-card sprint. Complete each round for points and use one strategic swap when a constraint does not fit.
items: Make the ending explain the beginning.
Remove the feature you would normally show first.
Use repetition as the organizing principle.
Design for someone using only one hand.
Let one deliberate imperfection remain visible.

apps.name: Signal Garden
apps.summary: Read harder moving signal patterns across four stations in practice, rush, or survival mode.
apps.description: A local pattern-prediction game with eight pattern families, adaptive difficulty, focus-powered hints, scoring, streaks, lives, timed play, canvas motion, touch, keyboard controls, optional audio, and clean teardown.

config.instructions: Watch signals move among four stations, infer the rule, and predict the next stop. Practice, rush, or survive ten adaptive rounds with focus hints.
prompts: Orbit signals move consistently around the garden.
Echo signals repeat each station before moving on.
Weave signals alternate between two independent paths.
Mirror signals fold back through a short motif.
Pulse signals return to one anchor between changes.
Skip signals leap over one station before settling.
Bloom signals expand outward, then collapse back inward.
Drift signals slow near one station before changing direction.

apps.name: Scene Circuit
apps.summary: Build a six-part scene, then add complications that drive it through reversals, costs, and an earned ending.
apps.description: A staged story lab with six ingredient lenses and a progression arc. Select a core cast, place, pressure, resource, twist, and tone, then add or swap complications to test alternate routes through the same scene.

config.instructions: Choose one chip from each lens to build the core scene. Then add complications one at a time to advance the story beat, or swap a chip to test a different route.
items: Pressure: nobody may name the real problem
Resource: a favor that must be repaid
prompts: Final pass: state the opening image, opposing goals, ticking pressure, decisive reversal, cost of success, final choice, and closing image.
Opening image: show the lead already doing something that reveals a flaw before anyone explains the problem.
Opposition: give two selected ingredients goals that cannot both succeed. Make the conflict visible through action rather than explanation.
Clock: turn the selected pressure into a countdown with a concrete consequence.
Rule and temptation: make one ingredient a rule, another the temptation to break it, and a third the price of doing so.
Living setting: let the place change what can be seen, heard, reached, or admitted. The setting must force a choice.
Core scene: combine the six selected lenses into a playable beginning, escalation, decision, and temporary result.
Escalation: add a complication that makes the current plan more attractive and more dangerous at the same time.
False win: let the characters solve the visible problem, then reveal the selected detail that proves the deeper problem remains.
Cost: assign a specific cost to success. Decide who pays it now and who discovers it later.
Reversal: make the least dramatic selected ingredient overturn the current interpretation without invalidating earlier clues.
Role pressure: force the lead and rival to exchange responsibilities.
Callback: repeat an earlier object, line, or movement with a changed meaning that exposes the emotional shift.
Close the easy exit: remove the obvious resolution and build a smaller, stranger option.
Truth beat: allow one honest sentence. It should solve the emotional question while making the practical problem harder.
Final choice: present two viable endings with different costs.
Closing image: echo the opening image with one precise difference that proves the scene changed something.
Remix audit: swap one chip and identify every beat that must change.
options: Twist: the warning was an invitation
Twist: the rival quietly needs help
Twist: the missing object is visible
Tone: tense with one calm person
Tone: formal behavior in a ridiculous situation

apps.name: Ripple Map
apps.summary: Build a consequence map that exposes delayed costs, feedback loops, hidden dependencies, and exit signals.
apps.description: A local systems-thinking lab for decisions with second-order effects. Add one lens at a time across choice, stakeholder, horizon, pressure, dependency, and signal; each selection advances the analysis toward a reversible test.

config.instructions: Select one chip from each lens in order: choice, stakeholder, horizon, pressure, dependency, and signal. Read the new consequence question after every selection.
prompts: Synthesize the whole map. State the decision, costs, delayed consequence, feedback loop, warning signal, and reversible test.
Immediate effect: what changes first if this choice is made, and what stays deceptively unchanged?
Distribution: who receives the visible benefit, and who quietly inherits extra work, risk, or waiting?
Time shift: how does the outcome look at this horizon compared with the first day?
Pressure response: what workaround appears, and what new risk does it create?
Dependency path: trace where this dependency can delay, distort, or concentrate the effects.
Signal and probe: turn the selected signal into a stop condition and a reversible test.
Work migration: identify work that appears to disappear but actually moves elsewhere.
Metric trap: name a number that could improve while the real experience worsens.
Feedback loop: describe one reinforcing loop after each cycle.
Delayed invoice: what cost arrives only after reversal becomes difficult?
Asymmetry: who can recover quickly from a bad outcome, and who cannot?
Lock-in: which habit, dependency, expectation, or data structure becomes harder to unwind?
Recovery design: what must remain intact so the team can retreat?
Missing voice: whose experience is absent from the selected lenses?
No-action comparison: compare this map with doing nothing.
Smaller option: redesign the choice as a bounded experiment.
Monitoring contract: define one leading signal, one lagging outcome, and a review date.

apps.name: Threadline
apps.summary: Route a live signal through costly terrain and ordered relays before its charge runs out.
apps.description: A procedural spatial strategy game with three modes, ordered checkpoints, terrain costs, optional caches, limited surges and hints, adaptive difficulty, scoring, canvas motion, drag, touch, pointer, keyboard controls, local audio, and clean teardown.

config.instructions: Trace a route from start to exit, linking numbered relays in order. Manage charge across costly terrain, scarce surges and focus.
prompts: Relays must be reached in number order before the exit unlocks.
Brush costs two charge and storm cells cost three.
A surge makes the next valid move cost zero charge.
Focus reveals up to three useful cells toward the next objective.

apps.name: Faultline Drill
apps.summary: Run a tabletop incident drill that exposes weak signals, tradeoffs, handoffs, and recovery paths.
apps.description: A local operational-resilience simulator. Build an incident from six lenses, contain the first failure, stress the response with alternate conditions, and finish with a concrete recovery and learning plan.

config.instructions: Choose one chip from each lens to form the initial incident. Then add or swap alternate conditions to test containment, escalation, recovery, and learning.
prompts: Final debrief: state the incident, earliest signal, blast radius, containment decision, tradeoff, recovery path, owner, and improvement.
Normal state: define healthy operation for the selected system and name one assumed dependency.
Detection: separate the first observable signal from the first interpretation.
Blast radius: identify who is affected now, who is affected next, and which boundary prevents spread.
First decision: choose one action that buys information and one action that reduces harm.
Scarce resource: assign the selected constraint to a named decision.
Containment plan: combine six selected lenses into a fifteen-minute response.
False recovery: assume the visible symptom clears while the cause remains.
Handoff risk: move control to another role or team.
Second-order failure: show how containment creates a new queue, dependency, or behavior.
Stakeholder conflict: give two stakeholders different definitions of success.
Metric trap: name a reassuring number that could improve while harm grows.
Escalation threshold: define exact evidence that changes the response.
No-regrets move: choose an action that remains useful across several causes.
Plan failure: remove one resource or add one constraint.
Recovery: describe the order for restoring service, validating data, and clearing workarounds.
Communication contract: write one internal update and one user-facing update.
Learning loop: convert one signal into an earlier alert and one handoff into a checklist.
options: Resource: trusted expert for ten minutes

apps.name: Truce Table
apps.summary: Stage a negotiation with leverage, concessions, hidden risks, and an agreement that survives pressure.
apps.description: A local negotiation simulator for messy group decisions. Build the table from roles, stakes, constraints, offers, risks, and trust signals, then pressure-test the agreement through concessions, walkaways, enforcement, and repair.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to form the negotiation table. Add or swap conditions to test concessions, walkaways, enforcement, repair, and final agreement.
prompts: Final agreement: state each party's real need, conceded item, protected boundary, enforcement signal, repair path, and next commitment.
Opening positions: list what each side says they want and the quieter need.
Power map: identify who can delay, veto, rescue, or undermine the agreement.
Constraint pressure: make the selected constraint force a real tradeoff.
First concession: choose a concession that creates trust without losing the boundary.
Walkaway line: define where continuing creates more damage than pausing.
Package deal: combine six selected lenses into one proposal.
Misread motive: assume one party misunderstands another's goal.
Hidden cost: reveal a cost that lands outside the room.
Trust test: design a small reversible action each party can complete.
Enforcement: define what happens when a promise slips.
Face-saving exit: create a path where someone can change position.
Fallback drill: remove one selected resource or offer.
Asymmetric risk: decide who loses fastest if the deal fails.
Signal review: choose the earliest signal that the agreement is working.
Repair move: assume trust drops after the first week.
No-deal comparison: compare the agreement with doing nothing.
Final audit: remove any promise without an owner, signal, or fallback.

apps.name: Throughput Foundry
apps.summary: Balance demand, capacity, quality, morale, and rework in a bottleneck simulation.
apps.description: A local operations-simulation lab. Select demand, bottleneck, policy, capacity, quality, and morale conditions, then stress-test queues, rework, tradeoffs, recovery, and learning loops.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to model the line. Add or swap conditions to test queues, rework, priority rules, morale, recovery, and throughput.
prompts: Final run: state the bottleneck, policy, queue behavior, quality risk, morale cost, recovery rule, and flow signal.
Baseline: describe one normal unit entering the line and where it waits.
Arrival pattern: show how the selected demand changes the queue.
Constraint map: name the true bottleneck and one tempting non-bottleneck improvement.
Policy test: apply the selected policy for one cycle.
Capacity tradeoff: add the selected capacity and name the new cost.
First run: combine six selected lenses into a one-day operating rule.
Queue spiral: assume waiting doubles and a shortcut appears.
Rework loop: reveal a defect that returns finished work to an earlier station.
Priority conflict: two jobs claim urgency.
Morale load: describe how the policy feels to the slowest station.
Batch risk: change the unit size and expose hidden problems.
Failure drill: remove one capacity chip or add one demand chip.
Flow experiment: set a short test with a work-in-progress limit.
Recovery sequence: decide what clears first after a jam.
Gaming check: name a metric people could improve while customers wait longer.
Learning loop: convert one queue signal into an earlier warning.
Final audit: remove any rule without an owner, signal, or rollback.

apps.name: Evidence Trail
apps.summary: Build and stress-test a case by weighing clues, alternatives, contradictions, and decisive tests.
apps.description: A local investigation lab for sharper reasoning. Select claims, sources, clues, timelines, contradictions, and tests, then pressure-check confidence, missing evidence, rival explanations, and revision paths.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to form the case. Add or swap evidence to test alternatives, confidence, missing facts, and what would change your mind.
prompts: Final case: state the claim, strongest evidence, weakest assumption, competing explanation, decisive test, and what would change your mind.
Case intake: translate the selected claim into a testable question.
Source quality: rate who produced the selected clue and what incentive might distort it.
Timeline: place the first three selected items in sequence.
Contradiction: identify two selected details that cannot both be fully true.
Alternative: build a rival explanation using the same evidence.
Core theory: combine six selected lenses into a provisional story.
Base-rate check: compare the theory with a boring common explanation.
Missing evidence: name one absent record, witness, measurement, or artifact.
Contamination risk: show how memory, incentives, or sampling could distort a clue.
Prediction: write one risky prediction the theory makes.
Disconfirmation: choose the evidence that would damage your favorite explanation most.
Chain of custody: trace how the selected clue moved from event to interpretation.
Confidence split: assign confidence to each major step.
Motive caution: propose a motive, then explain why motive alone is not evidence.
Decision threshold: decide whether the case supports action, inquiry, or dismissal.
Peer challenge: have a skeptical reviewer rewrite the case.
Revision audit: remove one selected chip and identify which conclusion changes.

apps.name: Heist Ledger
apps.summary: Plan a caper by balancing crew roles, heat, tools, clocks, suspicion, escape, and recovery.
apps.description: A local planning game about risk and contingency. Build a clean job, then pressure-test it through crew conflicts, alarms, evidence trails, failed tools, escape timing, and recovery choices.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to form the job. Add or swap complications to manage heat, crew trust, evidence, timing, escape, and the final split.
items: Tool: map with one old detail
prompts: Final ledger: state the target, crew role, clean entry, heat source, contingency, escape route, evidence risk, and split.
Crew read: assign each selected crew member one strength and pressure point.
Target map: describe the path in, the path out, and the loud failure point.
Tool test: use the selected tool for entry and name the trace it leaves.
Clock pressure: turn the selected clock into a countdown.
Heat check: name the first signal that someone notices the job.
Clean job: combine six selected lenses into a plan with entry, handoff, extraction, escape, and stop condition.
Crew conflict: one crew member protects themselves instead of the plan.
False vault: the visible prize is bait.
Silent alarm: assume the alarm already fired but nobody knows yet.
Evidence trail: identify the smallest trace that links the crew to the target.
Decoy move: create a harmless-looking event that draws attention away.
Tool failure: remove the selected tool and redesign the next two steps.
Split decision: decide whether the crew divides up, stays together, or abandons part of the prize.
Escape math: compare the fast exit, quiet exit, and unexpected exit.
Recovery: assume the job half-succeeds.
Double-cross audit: identify who benefits if the plan fails.
Final score: rate clean entry, heat control, crew trust, evidence risk, and escape margin.

apps.name: Loopwright
apps.summary: Design a replayable habit loop by tuning triggers, friction, rewards, setbacks, and recovery rules.
apps.description: A local behavior-design lab for routines, games, and product loops. Build a loop from trigger, action, friction, reward, identity, and setback chips, then stress-test relapse, boredom, escalation, and recovery.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to build the loop. Add or swap conditions to tune friction, reward timing, relapse recovery, and replay value.
prompts: Final loop: state the trigger, smallest action, friction guardrail, reward timing, setback rule, and next run hook.
Entry point: make the selected trigger visible without nagging.
Tiny action: shrink the first action until it can survive low energy.
Friction map: separate useful friction from accidental friction.
Reward timing: decide what feedback arrives immediately and later.
Identity cue: connect the loop to the kind of person the user is practicing becoming.
First loop: combine six selected lenses into a start, action, reward, recovery, and next step.
Miss recovery: assume the streak breaks.
Boredom drift: after repeated wins, what becomes too predictable?
Escalation check: when does making the loop harder help mastery?
Choice pressure: limit the next decision to two meaningful paths.
Social layer: add another person without surveillance, shame, or dependency.
Failure signal: name the earliest sign that the loop becomes performative.
Unlock rule: define one session-only unlock that rewards learning.
Resource tradeoff: add a cost in time, attention, or trust.
End condition: decide when to stop for the day.
Replay audit: swap one chip and identify which part of the loop changes.
Final tuning: remove any reward, metric, or pressure that reduces progress.

apps.name: Commons Keeper
apps.summary: Negotiate shared-resource survival by balancing harvest, repair, trust, enforcement, and shocks.
apps.description: A local commons-management strategy lab. Build a shared resource system, then pressure-test extraction, replenishment, free-riding, monitoring, repair costs, fairness, enforcement, and recovery.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to set the commons. Add or swap conditions to test harvest limits, repair, free-riding, enforcement, trust, and recovery.
prompts: Final charter: state the resource limit, fair-use rule, repair fund, monitoring signal, enforcement step, recovery path, and trust promise.
Baseline: describe one normal use cycle.
Limit design: set a harvest cap that protects the resource.
Fairness tension: decide who receives access first when demand exceeds supply.
Maintenance bill: assign repair work, money, or waiting time.
First charter: combine six selected lenses into a shared-use rule.
Free-rider test: assume one participant takes more than agreed.
Shock round: add one pressure and identify which rule breaks first.
Trust repair: after a violation, define the smallest useful repair.
Monitoring trap: name a metric that could look fair while the resource declines.
Exit option: decide what happens when someone cannot follow the rule.
Scarcity spiral: show how fear of shortage makes overuse more likely.
Enforcement ladder: separate reminder, repair, restriction, and removal.
Newcomer path: add a participant who did not help create the rule.
Recovery season: after damage, decide what stops and what continues.
Final audit: remove any rule without an owner, signal, repair path, or mercy clause.

apps.name: Launch Window
apps.summary: Pressure-test a product rollout across audience, promise, channel, metric, risk, and rollback choices.
apps.description: A local product-launch simulator for go/no-go planning. Build a rollout from audience, promise, channel, constraint, metric, and risk chips, then test sequencing, adoption, feedback, rollback, trust, and learning.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to shape the rollout. Add or swap conditions to test launch order, adoption, feedback, rollback, and trust.
prompts: Final launch plan: state the audience, promise, release path, success metric, risk, rollback trigger, owner, and next checkpoint.
Audience fit: describe who benefits first and who might be confused.
Promise test: turn the selected promise into one observable behavior.
Channel choice: decide why this release path is safer or faster.
Constraint pressure: show how the selected constraint limits support, timing, training, or scope.
Metric line: choose one leading signal and one lagging outcome.
First rollout: combine six selected lenses into a go/no-go plan.
Adoption drag: assume the right users do not notice or understand the change.
Feedback split: separate loud complaints from representative evidence.
Rollback drill: define exactly what reverses, what stays, and what data is preserved.
Trust cost: identify who pays the cost of a failed launch.
Scope cut: remove one feature, audience, or channel.
Training gap: show what someone must know before launch day.
Support surge: assume help requests double.
Competitive pressure: add urgency without pretending uncertainty disappeared.
Learning loop: convert one launch signal into a follow-up experiment.
Expansion gate: decide what evidence permits the next cohort.
Final audit: remove any claim, metric, or promise without an owner and rollback path.

apps.name: Service Rhythm
apps.summary: Keep a tiny restaurant alive by balancing stations, tickets, supplies, patience, and recovery.
apps.description: A local dinner-rush strategy lab. Build a service from stations, tickets, staff, prep, guests, and shocks, then pressure-test timing, bottlenecks, substitutions, mistakes, morale, and comeback plans.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to set the rush. Add or swap complications to manage tickets, station load, guest patience, supplies, recovery, and final service score.
prompts: Final service: state the bottleneck, ticket promise, station rule, substitution, guest signal, morale cost, recovery move, and score.
Door open: describe the first ticket, who touches it, and where delay will quietly start.
Station map: identify the real bottleneck and one tempting fix that only moves the jam elsewhere.
Ticket promise: decide what must be perfect, what may be slow, and what can be simplified without breaking trust.
Prep tradeoff: use the selected prep condition. What gets stretched, substituted, or removed from the menu?
Staff rhythm: assign the selected staff role one decision they own and one mistake they are likely to catch too late.
First rush: combine six selected lenses into a ten-minute service plan with owner, queue signal, limit, and recovery trigger.
Patience drop: assume guests wait twice as long. What apology, sample, or visible action buys honest time?
Mistake fire: one finished plate returns. Which station absorbs rework, and what ticket now risks failing?
Supply shock: remove one prep chip or add one ticket chip. Redesign the next three orders without pretending capacity stayed the same.
Morale check: show how the rule feels to the slowest station after repeated cycles. What signal appears before burnout?
Expo call: choose which ticket moves first when every order claims urgency. State the cost accepted out loud.
Substitution test: create a swap that protects safety and trust without hiding the shortage.
Clean reset: decide what stops for two minutes so the room can recover instead of compounding errors.
Guest split: one table wants speed, one wants care, one wants explanation. What message fits each without overpromising?
Late rush: add a new ticket after recovery begins. Which rule prevents a second spiral?
Learning ticket: convert one failure into a prep checklist, one bottleneck into a limit, and one apology into an earlier signal.
Final audit: remove any promise without an owner, visible signal, fallback, or safety boundary before replaying.
